questions.specification: A university stores its research data on a private cloud, which is accessible only by faculty and students.

parts.content: Define cloud computing and outline its difference from traditional client-server architecture.
parts.markscheme:
Cloud computing is the delivery of on-demand computing services (such as processing and storage) over the internet <marks>A1</marks>

In a traditional client-server model, data and applications are stored on specific local hardware/physical servers owned and managed by the organization <marks>A1</marks>

In contrast, cloud computing uses virtualized resources that are abstracted from the physical hardware, allowing for easier remote access and scalability <marks>A1</marks>

[3 marks total]

parts.content: Explain two benefits of using a private cloud for the university’s research data.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>2 marks</marks> for each benefit explained, up to a maximum of <marks>4 marks</marks>.

**Enhanced Security and Privacy**
A private cloud is dedicated to a single organization, meaning the university has full control over security protocols <marks>A1</marks>. This ensures that sensitive research data is protected from external access or multi-tenant vulnerabilities found in public clouds <marks>A1</marks>.

**Customization and Control**
The university can tailor the cloud infrastructure to meet specific research needs, such as specialized software environments or hardware configurations <marks>A1</marks>. This level of control is often not possible with public cloud providers that offer standardized services <marks>A1</marks>.

**Performance and Reliability**
Because the infrastructure is not shared with other organizations, the university has dedicated bandwidth and processing power <marks>A1</marks>. This ensures that large-scale data analysis or simulations are not slowed down by external network traffic <marks>A1</marks>.

[4 marks total]

steps.title: Understand the Private Cloud
steps.content: A **private cloud** is a cloud computing environment dedicated solely to one organization, in this case, the university. Unlike a public cloud where resources are shared among many users (multi-tenancy), a private cloud offers an isolated environment. This distinction is crucial for understanding how it enhances security for sensitive research data, a concept explored in **Topic C.4: The evolving web**.

steps.title: Full Control Over Security
steps.content:
The first major benefit is that the university has **full control over security protocols**. Because the infrastructure is not managed by a third-party provider with standardized rules, the university can implement specific encryption standards, firewalls, and access levels that meet the exact needs of their research projects. 

Award 1 mark for identifying control over protocols and 1 mark for explaining how this protects sensitive data from unauthorized external access.

highlights.text: university has full control over security protocols

steps.title: Isolation From Shared Risks
steps.content:
The second benefit is the **isolation of data from multi-tenant risks**. In a public cloud, your data might reside on the same physical server as another company's data. A private cloud eliminates vulnerabilities associated with sharing hardware, such as data leakage between users or "side-channel" attacks. 

Award 1 mark for mentioning the isolation from multi-tenant environments and 1 mark for explaining that this prevents data from being exposed to vulnerabilities found in public clouds.

highlights.text: multi-tenant vulnerabilities found in public clouds

steps.title: Final Summary of Marks
steps.content:
To maximize your marks (up to 4), ensure you connect the feature of the private cloud to a specific security outcome:

1.  **Feature:** Full control over security $\rightarrow$ **Outcome:** Tailored protection for sensitive research (2 marks).
2.  **Feature:** Isolated environment $\rightarrow$ **Outcome:** Protection from risks of shared (multi-tenant) hardware (2 marks).

steps.title: Understand the Private Cloud
steps.content: Before looking at the benefits, let's clarify what a private cloud is. Unlike a public cloud (like Google Drive or AWS), a **private cloud** is infrastructure dedicated solely to one organization. For a university, this means the hardware and software are not shared with any other external users, giving them a high degree of flexibility.

highlights.text: stores its research data on a private cloud

steps.title: Benefit 1: Specialized Customization
steps.content:
The university can customize every layer of the cloud stack. Research often requires very specific technical environments that public clouds might not support.

*   **Hardware:** They can install specialized hardware, such as high-end GPUs for AI research or massive amounts of RAM for complex simulations.
*   **Software:** They can run niche, legacy, or highly customized operating systems and scientific libraries that are not available in a standardized public cloud environment.

highlights.text: tailor the cloud infrastructure to meet specific research needs, such as specialized software environments or hardware configurations

steps.title: Benefit 2: Complete Administrative Control
steps.content:
Because the university owns and manages the infrastructure, they have total control over security and performance management. 

*   **Security:** They can implement their own strict encryption protocols and firewall rules to protect sensitive intellectual property, ensuring the data is isolated from external "multi-tenant" risks (risks from other users on the same server).
*   **Performance:** They can prioritize bandwidth and processing power for specific high-priority research projects without worrying about "noisy neighbors" (other companies) slowing down the network.

highlights.text: university has full control over security protocols

steps.title: Final Summary for Marks
steps.content:
To earn the full 4 marks on an IB exam, you must provide a benefit and then **explain** it. Here is how you should structure your answer:

1.  **Customization (2 marks):** The university can tailor hardware and software to meet unique research needs, which is often impossible with standardized public cloud providers.
2.  **Control (2 marks):** The university has full control over security protocols and dedicated resources, protecting sensitive data from external vulnerabilities and ensuring high performance for data analysis.

steps.title: Identify the performance-focused benefit
steps.content:
In a private cloud environment, the university doesn't share its physical infrastructure with other organizations. This is often referred to as a "single-tenant" architecture. 

The first major benefit is **Dedicated Bandwidth**. Because the network resources are reserved exclusively for the university, researchers can transfer massive datasets without competing for speed with external users or public internet traffic. 

This relates to **Topic 3: Networks (3.1.5 Speed variations)**, where we learn how shared traffic can cause bottlenecks.

highlights.text: infrastructure is not shared with other organizations

steps.title: Explain impact on processing
steps.content:
To earn the marks, we must explain the outcome of having dedicated resources. 

With **dedicated bandwidth**, large-scale research tasks (like data backups or real-time collaborations) are not slowed down by external network traffic. In a public cloud, a sudden spike in another company's usage could "choke" your connection; in a private cloud, your performance remains high and consistent.

highlights.text: not slowed down by external network traffic

steps.title: Analyze reliability for simulations
steps.content:
The second benefit is **Dedicated Processing Power (Reliability)**. Research often involves complex simulations or heavy data analysis that requires significant CPU and RAM resources. 

Because the university has full control over the hardware, these processes are **reliable**. They won't be interrupted or delayed by the processing demands of other "tenants" on the same server, which is a common issue in public cloud environments (the "noisy neighbor" effect). 

This connects to **Topic 6: Resource Management**, specifically how dedicated resources ensure predictable system performance.

highlights.text: dedicated bandwidth and processing power

steps.title: Finalize for full marks
steps.content:
To get the full 4 marks, ensure you clearly link the **resource** to the **result**:

1.  **Benefit 1 (Performance):** The university has dedicated bandwidth ($A1$), which ensures that large-scale data analysis or simulations are not slowed down by external network traffic ($A1$).
2.  **Benefit 2 (Reliability):** By using a private cloud, the university has dedicated processing power ($A1$). This ensures that heavy computational research tasks remain reliable and consistent regardless of external demand ($A1$).

parts.content: Describe one disadvantage of a private cloud model for this university.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for identifying a disadvantage and up to <marks>2 marks</marks> for description/development.

**High Total Cost of Ownership**
Unlike public clouds, the university is responsible for all costs associated with the infrastructure, including hardware, facility space, and power/cooling <marks>A1</marks>. This requires a significant upfront capital investment to build the system <marks>A1</marks>. Furthermore, the university must hire specialized IT staff to maintain and upgrade the private cloud, increasing long-term operational expenses <marks>A1</marks>.

**Limited Scalability (compared to public cloud)**
If research needs suddenly exceed current capacity, the university cannot instantly scale up as they would with a public provider <marks>A1</marks>. They would need to purchase and install new physical hardware <marks>A1</marks>, which causes delays and requires careful long-term resource planning <marks>A1</marks>.

[3 marks total]

steps.title: Identify the financial burden
steps.content: Using the **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** approach, the primary disadvantage for the university is the significantly higher financial responsibility compared to using a public cloud. While a public cloud uses a "pay-as-you-go" model, a private cloud requires the university to bear all costs for the infrastructure and its upkeep.

highlights.text: High Total Cost of Ownership

steps.title: Initial capital expenditure (CapEx)
steps.content: To establish a private cloud, the university must commit to a **significant upfront capital investment**. This involves purchasing expensive physical hardware, such as high-performance servers and storage arrays, as well as securing dedicated facility space with the necessary power and cooling systems.

highlights.text: significant upfront capital investment to build the system

steps.title: Ongoing operational expenses (OpEx)
steps.content: The costs don't stop after the initial setup. The university must also cover high **operational expenses**, which include hiring **specialized IT staff** to maintain, update, and secure the private cloud. These long-term staffing and maintenance costs contribute to a much higher TCO over the system's lifetime.

highlights.text: hire specialized IT staff to maintain and upgrade the private cloud

steps.title: Identify Limited Scalability
steps.content: A significant disadvantage of a private cloud model is its **limited scalability**. Unlike public clouds, which can appear to have infinite resources, a private cloud is strictly confined by the university's owned physical infrastructure.

highlights.text: Limited Scalability (compared to public cloud)

steps.title: Hardware Limitations
steps.content: To increase storage or processing power, the university cannot simply toggle a setting. Instead, they must **manually purchase and install new physical hardware**, such as servers or hard drives. This requires physical space, power, and human labor to integrate into the existing network.

highlights.text: purchase and install new physical hardware

steps.title: Impact on Research Agility
steps.content: This physical dependency causes **significant delays**. If a research project suddenly generates a massive amount of data, the university lacks the **elasticity** to respond instantly. The time taken for procurement and setup means the university is less agile and may face downtime or data bottlenecks while waiting for upgrades.

highlights.text: causes delays and requires careful long-term resource planning

steps.title: Identify the disadvantage
steps.content: A significant disadvantage of the private cloud model is the **high operational responsibility** placed on the university. Unlike a public cloud, where the provider manages the background infrastructure, a private cloud requires the university to handle all technical and administrative tasks internally.

highlights.text: private cloud

steps.title: Explain maintenance and security
steps.content: The university must take full responsibility for **security patches, software updates, and hardware repairs**. This includes manually monitoring for vulnerabilities and ensuring that all server components are functioning correctly. If a hardware component fails, the university cannot rely on an external provider; they must diagnose and fix the issue themselves to prevent data loss or downtime.

steps.title: Analyze human resource costs
steps.content: Because of this burden, the university must hire and retain **specialized IT personnel**. These dedicated human resources represent a significant management overhead and a continuous operational expense. This contrasts with public clouds where these costs are shared across thousands of customers, making the private model much more expensive to maintain over time.

highlights.text: hire specialized IT staff to maintain and upgrade the private cloud, increasing long-term operational expenses

steps.title: Summarize for marks
steps.content:
In summary, to earn the 3 marks, you should structure your answer as follows:
1.  **Identify**: The university has full operational responsibility for the cloud infrastructure (1 mark).
2.  **Describe**: They must manage all security updates and hardware maintenance internally (1 mark).
3.  **Develop**: This requires hiring specialized staff, leading to high ongoing human resource costs and management overhead (1 mark).

questions.specification:
VoyageDirect is a travel agency launching a new flight search portal. The development team is focusing on ensuring the site is optimized for search engines, maintainable, and provides a consistent user experience. Consider the following snippet from the site's search page and the structure of a typical API request used by the application. File name: `search.html` ```html

Find Your Next Adventure

``` Consider the following URL generated when a user filters their search results: `https://api.voyagedirect.com/v1/flights?origin=LHR&sort=price_asc` The World Wide Web Consortium (W3C) defines standards that allow the web to function as a universal medium for information exchange.

parts.content:
The developers must decide between using the HTTP `GET` and `POST` methods for submitting the booking form shown in the HTML snippet. 

Explain **two** reasons why the `POST` method is more appropriate than `GET` for submitting sensitive booking information.
parts.markscheme:
- Data sent via POST is included in the body of the request rather than the URL, preventing it from appearing in browser history or server logs <marks>1</marks>
- POST has no technical limit on data length, whereas GET is restricted by the maximum URL length allowed by browsers/servers <marks>1</marks>
- Using POST prevents data from being bookmarked, which is safer for transactions that should not be repeated accidentally <marks>1</marks>
- POST is considered 'non-idempotent' or a 'write' operation, making it structurally more suited for actions that change state on the server (like creating a booking) <marks>1</marks>

4 marks total

<note>Award [2] for identifying the reasons and [2] for the explanation/impact.</note>

parts.content: Describe how search engine spiders (web crawlers) would discover and index the content of the VoyageDirect website.
parts.markscheme:
- Spiders start with a list of known URLs (seeds) and visit the VoyageDirect homepage <marks>1</marks>
- The spider parses the HTML to find hyperlinks (`<a>` tags) and adds them to a queue to visit recursively <marks>1</marks>
- The content of the pages is scraped, and keywords/metadata are extracted to be stored in the search engine's database (the index) <marks>1</marks>
- The spider checks the `robots.txt` file of the site to identify which directories are permitted or restricted for crawling <marks>1</marks>

4 marks total

parts.content:
VoyageDirect uses an external CSS file (`theme.css`) instead of placing styles directly within the HTML elements. 

Explain **two** advantages of using an external stylesheet for a website with hundreds of pages.
parts.markscheme:
**Maintenance/Consistency**
- Changes to the design (e.g., changing the corporate font) only need to be made in one file to update the entire site <marks>1</marks>
- This ensures a consistent look and feel across all 100+ pages without manual editing of each file <marks>1</marks>

**Performance/Efficiency**
- External CSS files are cached by the browser after the first visit <marks>1</marks>
- This reduces the amount of data transferred for subsequent page loads, improving site speed and reducing bandwidth costs <marks>1</marks>

4 marks total

parts.content:
Analyze the structure of the following URL provided in the specification: 
`https://api.voyagedirect.com/v1/flights?origin=LHR&sort=price_asc` 

Describe the function of the **four** distinct components of this URL.
parts.markscheme:
- `https`: The protocol used for secure communication between the client and server <marks>1</marks>
- `api.voyagedirect.com`: The domain name/host identifying the specific server where the resource resides <marks>1</marks>
- `/v1/flights`: The path identifying the specific resource or endpoint on the server <marks>1</marks>
- `?origin=LHR&sort=price_asc`: The query string/parameters used to pass specific data (filters) to the server-side script <marks>1</marks>

4 marks total

parts.content: The HTTP protocol is described as 'stateless'. Explain how cookies are used by the VoyageDirect server to overcome this limitation and maintain a user's flight 'wishlist' across different pages.
parts.markscheme:
- Because HTTP is stateless, the server does not remember the user from one request to the next <marks>1</marks>
- When the user adds a flight, the server sends a `Set-Cookie` header in the response containing a unique session ID <marks>1</marks>
- The browser stores this cookie and automatically sends it back to the server with every subsequent request <marks>1</marks>
- The server uses the ID to look up the user's specific data in its backend database, creating the illusion of a continuous session <marks>1</marks>

4 marks total

parts.content: Identify **two** ways in which the use of the `alt` attribute in the `` tag improves the accessibility of the website.
parts.markscheme:
- It provides a text description that can be read aloud by screen readers for visually impaired users <marks>1</marks>
- It provides context or information if the image fails to load due to a poor connection or broken link <marks>1</marks>

2 marks total

questions.specification:
GlobalNewsHub.net is a news aggregation platform that uses automated web crawlers to index headlines from various international sources. The site allows users to customize their dashboard by selecting specific categories such as 'Technology' or 'Environment'. 

To provide a responsive user experience, the platform utilizes a mix of client-side and server-side processing. When a user navigates to the site using the URL `https://www.GlobalNewsHub.net`, their browser must resolve the address and retrieve the necessary files. The site structure uses organized directories, and developers use relative paths for internal navigation to ensure the site remains portable across different hosting environments.

parts.content: Explain how a web crawler (spider) discovers and indexes new pages on the World Wide Web, and how a developer can influence which parts of a site are crawled.
parts.markscheme:
### Method #1

- The crawler starts with a list of known URLs (seed URLs) and visits them to identify all hyperlinks on those pages. <marks>1</marks>
- It recursively follows these links to discover new pages, adding them to a search engine's index/database. <marks>1</marks>
- Developers can influence this by using a `robots.txt` file to specify which directories or files should be ignored (disallowed) by the crawler. <marks>1</marks>
- Meta tags (e.g., `<meta name="robots" content="noindex">`) can also be embedded in the HTML header to prevent specific pages from appearing in search results. <marks>1</marks>

<note>Award up to [4 max]</note>

parts.content: Outline the sequence of events that occurs when a browser resolves the URL `https://www.GlobalNewsHub.net` to retrieve the web page from the server.
parts.markscheme:
### Method #1

1. The browser checks its cache for the IP address; if not found, it sends a request to a DNS (Domain Name System) server to translate the domain name into an IP address. <marks>1</marks>
2. Once the IP is obtained, the browser establishes a TCP connection (often via a TLS handshake for HTTPS) with the server at that address. <marks>1</marks>
3. The browser sends an HTTP GET request to the server, which then processes the request and sends back the HTML files and associated resources (CSS, JS). <marks>1</marks>

parts.content: Distinguish between an absolute URL and a relative URL, providing an example of how a relative link would function within a sub-directory.
parts.markscheme:
### Method #1

- An **absolute URL** provides the full address including the protocol and domain (e.g., `https://www.GlobalNewsHub.net/images/logo.png`), whereas a **relative URL** specifies the path in relation to the current page's location. <marks>1</marks>
- **Example**: If a user is on `www.GlobalNewsHub.net/tech/index.html` and a relative link is written as `<a href="../contact.html">`, the browser will look one directory up for the `contact.html` file at the root level. <marks>1</marks>

### Method #1

- Award <marks>1</marks> for identifying that the `onsubmit` event triggers, and `event.preventDefault()` stops the page from refreshing/submitting to a server.
- Award <marks>1</marks> for the logical operation of `getStats` on the input string:
    - `trim()` removes any leading/trailing whitespace.
    - `split(/\s+/)` breaks the string into an array of words: `["The", "web", "is", "an", "evolving", "ecosystem", "of", "interconnected", "data."]`.
    - `count` becomes 9.
    - `9 / 200` is calculated and `Math.ceil()` rounds it up to `1`.
- Award <marks>1</marks> for identifying the final output string generation and its insertion into the HTML element: `Count: 9 | Time: 1 min`.

<note>Award up to [3 max]</note>

parts.content: Evaluate the use of client-side scripting to perform data validation on a web form before the data is sent to the server.
parts.markscheme:
### Method #1

**Advantages of Client-Side Validation:**
- **Immediate Feedback**: Users receive instant alerts about errors (e.g., missing fields) without waiting for a server response, improving the user experience. <marks>1</marks>
- **Reduced Server Load**: Invalid requests are caught early, preventing the server from wasting processing power or bandwidth on malformed data. <marks>1</marks>

**Disadvantages of Client-Side Validation:**
- **Security Risk**: Client-side scripts can be bypassed or disabled by a malicious user in their browser settings or via the console; therefore, it cannot be the only line of defense. <marks>1</marks>
- **Browser Dependency**: If a user has JavaScript disabled or is using an extremely old browser, the validation logic may not execute at all. <marks>1</marks>

<note>Award up to [4 max]</note>

questions.specification: An e-commerce company, ShopEase, operates a global online marketplace where users can buy and sell products. To protect user data, ShopEase employs identification and authentication protocols.

parts.content: Define identification and authentication in the context of online security.
parts.markscheme:
Identification: The process of a user claiming a unique identity to a system, such as by entering a username <marks>A1</marks>

Authentication: The process of verifying that the person claiming the identity is who they say they are, typically through a password or biometric scan <marks>A1</marks>

Together, these processes ensure that access is restricted to authorized users and protect account integrity <marks>A1</marks>

Total [3 marks]

steps.title: Defining Identification
steps.content:
In computer science, **identification** is the process where a user or system first declares who they are. It is essentially the act of claiming a unique identity. 

For a platform like ShopEase, this occurs when a user enters a piece of information that is unique to them within the system, such as a **username** or an **account ID**. This step tells the system which account is being accessed, but it does not yet prove that the person entering the ID is the actual owner.

highlights.text: The process of a user claiming a unique identity to a system, such as by entering a username

steps.title: Defining Authentication
steps.content:
Once an identity has been claimed, the system must verify it. **Authentication** is the technical process of proving that the person claiming the identity is indeed who they say they are.

This is typically achieved by requiring the user to provide "factors" that only the true owner should possess. Common examples include:
*   **Knowledge factors:** Something you know (e.g., a password or PIN).
*   **Ownership factors:** Something you have (e.g., a security token or mobile phone).
*   **Inherence factors:** Something you are (e.g., a biometric scan like a fingerprint).

highlights.text: The process of verifying that the person claiming the identity is who they say they are, typically through a password or biometric scan

steps.title: The Combined Purpose
steps.content:
While identification and authentication are distinct steps, they work together as a single security protocol. 

The combined goal of these processes is to ensure that system resources and private data are **restricted to authorized users only**. By requiring both a claim (identification) and proof (authentication), ShopEase can protect the integrity of user accounts and prevent unauthorized access by malicious actors.

highlights.text: Together, these processes ensure that access is restricted to authorized users and protect account integrity

steps.title: Step 1: Claiming an Identity
steps.content:
The first part of the ShopEase login process is **identification**. This is where a user makes a claim about who they are to the system. 

In a procedural sense, this is like typing your **username** into the login box. At this stage, ShopEase knows which account is being referenced, but it hasn't verified if you are actually the owner of that account yet.

highlights.text: The process of a user claiming a unique identity to a system, such as by entering a username

steps.title: Step 2: Proving the Claim
steps.content:
Once the system knows who you claim to be, it moves to the next procedural step: **authentication**. This is the process of verifying that the person claiming the identity is indeed the authorized user.

Following the ShopEase example, this happens when you provide a **password**, a **biometric scan** (like FaceID), or a one-time code. The system compares this secret information against its records to see if it matches the identity claimed in Step 1.

highlights.text: The process of verifying that the person claiming the identity is who they say they are, typically through a password or biometric scan

steps.title: The Goal: Security and Integrity
steps.content:
The reason these two steps work in sequence is to provide **security**. By requiring both a claim (identification) and proof (authentication), ShopEase ensures that access is restricted to authorized users only.

This procedural flow protects **account integrity**, meaning it prevents unauthorized individuals from accessing sensitive personal data or making purchases on someone else's behalf.

highlights.text: Together, these processes ensure that access is restricted to authorized users and protect account integrity

parts.content: Explain one method of identification and one method of authentication that ShopEase might use.
parts.markscheme:
**Identification (max 2 marks):**
- Method: Email addresses or unique usernames <marks>A1</marks>
- Explanation: These serve as a unique primary key in the database, allowing ShopEase to distinguish one individual's account and data from millions of others <marks>A1</marks>

**Authentication (max 2 marks):**
- Method: Multi-factor authentication (MFA) <marks>A1</marks>
- Explanation: It requires a second form of proof (such as a one-time code sent to a mobile device), ensuring that even if a password is stolen, the account remains secure as the attacker lacks the physical device <marks>A1</marks>

*(Note: Accept other valid methods like passwords or biometrics if a clear explanation of how they verify identity is provided for the second mark)*

Total [4 marks]

steps.title: Distinguish between the two concepts
steps.content:
Before choosing the methods, it is important to understand the difference between these two security steps:

*   **Identification**: The process of a user claiming a specific identity (telling the system "who" they are).
*   **Authentication**: The process of the system verifying that the user actually is who they claim to be (proving the identity).

In the **Standard Account Credentials** approach, we use a pairing of a unique identifier and a secret credential.

steps.title: Choose an Identification Method
steps.content: A common method for identification at ShopEase would be the use of **unique usernames** or **email addresses**.

highlights.text: Email addresses or unique usernames

steps.title: Explain Identification
steps.content: The username or email address serves as a **primary key** in ShopEase's database. Because it is unique to every account, it allows the system to distinguish one specific user's data (like their order history and shipping address) from millions of other users in the global marketplace.

highlights.text: primary key

steps.title: Choose an Authentication Method
steps.content: Using the Standard Account Credentials approach, the method ShopEase would use for authentication is a **password**.

steps.title: Explain Authentication
steps.content: A password acts as a secret known only to the user. When a user logs in, ShopEase compares the entered password against the one stored in their database. This **verifies identity** because it assumes that only the authorized owner of the account would know that specific secret string of characters.

highlights.text: verify identity

steps.title: Define Identification and Authentication
steps.content:
To answer this question correctly, we first need to distinguish between the two concepts:

*   **Identification**: The process of a user claiming an identity (answering "Who are you?").
*   **Authentication**: The process of proving that identity (answering "Prove it!").

This question is related to **Topic 3.1.11 (Network Security Methods)** and **Topic C.4.5 (Privacy and Authentication)**.

steps.title: Identification: Unique Email Address
steps.content:
A common and effective method of identification is using a **unique email address**.

**Explanation:**
In the backend database of ShopEase, the email address acts as a **primary key**. This ensures that every account is distinct. When a user enters their email, the system uses it to locate and retrieve that specific individual's data (like their order history and shipping address) from millions of other records.

$$\text{Identification Mark: Method (1) + Explanation (1)}$$

highlights.text: Email addresses or unique usernames
These serve as a unique primary key in the database

steps.title: Authentication: Multi-factor Authentication (MFA)
steps.content:
Following the **Advanced Verification Approach**, a robust method of authentication is **Multi-factor Authentication (MFA)**.

**Explanation:**
MFA requires the user to provide two or more forms of evidence. For example, after entering a password (something they know), ShopEase might send a one-time passcode (OTP) to the user's registered mobile device (something they have). This adds a critical layer of security; even if a hacker steals a password, they cannot access the account without physical possession of the user's phone.

$$\text{Authentication Mark: Method (1) + Explanation (1)}$$

highlights.text: Multi-factor authentication (MFA)
ensuring that even if a password is stolen, the account remains secure as the attacker lacks the physical device

steps.title: Final Answer Structure
steps.content:
To secure the full 4 marks, your response should look like this:

1.  **Identification (2 marks):** Use a **unique email address**. This acts as a primary key in the ShopEase database, allowing the system to distinguish one specific user's account from all others.
2.  **Authentication (2 marks):** Use **Multi-factor Authentication (MFA)**. This requires a second form of proof, such as a code sent to a smartphone, ensuring the account stays safe even if the password is compromised.

parts.content: Describe one potential impact on user privacy when both identification and authentication are required on ShopEase.
parts.markscheme:
Impact: **Increased risk of personal data exposure**
- Identification and authentication require the collection and storage of sensitive personal data (e.g., phone numbers for MFA or biometric data) <marks>A1</marks>
- If ShopEase suffers a security breach, this highly personal information could be leaked to unauthorized parties <marks>A1</marks>
- This leads to a permanent loss of privacy, as compromised identifiers like biometrics cannot be changed and may be used to track the user across other services <marks>A1</marks>

Total [3 marks]

steps.title: Identify data collection requirements
steps.content:
To implement both **identification** and **authentication**, ShopEase must collect and store highly sensitive personal information. This includes high-value credentials such as phone numbers for multi-factor authentication (MFA) or biometric data (like fingerprints or facial recognition patterns) used for identity verification.

This is the first step toward a privacy impact because the mere existence of this data in a database creates a potential target.

highlights.text: identification and authentication

steps.title: Assess the breach risk
steps.content:
Using a **Data Security Risk Approach**, we recognize that storing these credentials increases the 'attack surface' for ShopEase. If the platform suffers a security breach or an internal leak, this sensitive information could be accessed by unauthorized parties.

Because this data is directly linked to a user's physical or digital identity, its exposure is far more damaging than the loss of a simple username.

steps.title: Evaluate the permanent impact
steps.content:
The most significant impact on privacy is the **permanence of the loss**. Unlike a password, which can be reset after a leak, biometric data or phone numbers are difficult or impossible to change.

If these are stolen, the user's privacy is permanently compromised, as hackers can use these fixed identifiers to track or impersonate the user across other services indefinitely.

steps.title: Summary for Markscheme
steps.content:
To earn full marks, ensure your description links the collection of data to the specific risk of a breach and the resulting permanent privacy loss:

1. **Collection**: ShopEase must store sensitive data like biometrics or phone numbers.
2. **Exposure**: A security breach leads to this data leaking to unauthorized parties.
3. **Permanence**: Compromised biometrics cannot be changed, leading to a permanent loss of privacy and potential cross-service tracking.

steps.title: Link identity to actions
steps.content: In the **Loss of Anonymity Approach**, we focus on how requiring both identification and authentication prevents a user from interacting with the platform privately. By making these protocols mandatory, ShopEase forces users to provide sensitive personal data (such as phone numbers for Multi-Factor Authentication or biometric data like fingerprints). This creates a direct link between the user's real-world identity and their online behavior.

highlights.text: identification and authentication

steps.title: Stored data and vulnerability
steps.content: To maintain these protocols, ShopEase must store this highly personal information on their servers. This leads to an **increased risk of personal data exposure**. If ShopEase experience a security breach, the specific data used to prove who the user is—which is much more sensitive than a simple username—could be leaked to unauthorized parties.

steps.title: Permanent loss of privacy
steps.content: The ultimate impact is a **permanent loss of privacy**. Because identifiers like biometric data or real-world names cannot be easily changed (unlike a password), a leak means the user can never truly be anonymous again. This data can then be used to track the user across other services or build a deep profile of their habits without their consent.

steps.title: Summary of marks
steps.content:
To earn the full 3 marks, your description should include:

*   **1 Mark**: Identifying that sensitive personal data (e.g., biometrics or phone numbers) must be collected and stored.
*   **1 Mark**: Explaining that a security breach would leak this specific, highly personal info to unauthorized parties.
*   **1 Mark**: Describing the permanent impact, such as the inability to change biometrics and the resulting ability for the user to be tracked indefinitely.

questions.specification: ConnectCo, a global communications company, is exploring a decentralized web model to provide internet services to users in areas with unreliable infrastructure. ConnectCo envisions a decentralized model where individual devices can act as network nodes to improve connectivity.

parts.content: Define decentralized web and explain its relevance to ConnectCo’s model.
parts.markscheme:
- Define a decentralized web as one that operates without a central authority, relying on independent nodes to store and share data. <marks>A1</marks>
- Explain that individual devices act as network nodes, promoting connectivity in areas lacking infrastructure. <marks>A1</marks>
- Explain that this increases reliability by enabling local devices to maintain data access when the broader internet is unavailable. <marks>A1</marks>

[3 marks total]

steps.title: Define Decentralized Web
steps.content: To understand ConnectCo's approach, we first need to define a **decentralized web**. Unlike the traditional web which relies on central servers (like those owned by Google or Amazon), a decentralized web operates without a central authority. It uses a peer-to-peer (P2P) architecture where independent **nodes** are responsible for storing and sharing data across the network.

steps.title: Application to Infrastructure
steps.content: ConnectCo applies this definition by turning individual user devices into network nodes. This is crucial for areas with **unreliable infrastructure** because it allows devices to bypass the need for centralized cell towers or physical cables. If one device has data, it can pass it directly to another nearby device, effectively building a network from the ground up using the hardware already present in the community.

highlights.text: individual devices can act as network nodes
unreliable infrastructure

steps.title: Ensuring Network Reliability
steps.content:
Finally, this model significantly increases reliability. In a centralized system, if the main trunk line or server fails, the whole area loses access. In ConnectCo’s decentralized model, because data is distributed across local nodes, users can maintain access to shared data and communicate locally even when the connection to the broader global internet is interrupted. 

This maps to the **Topic C.4.8: Decentralized and Democratic Web** section of your syllabus, which explores how moving away from monopolies can improve service resilience.

steps.title: Identify the primary problem
steps.content: To understand ConnectCo's approach, we first look at the problem they are trying to solve: **unreliable infrastructure**. In a traditional (centralized) network, users depend on a central server or service provider. If the central hub fails or the cables are damaged, everyone loses access because there is a single point of failure.

highlights.text: unreliable infrastructure

steps.title: Define the decentralized solution
steps.content:
ConnectCo's solution is a **decentralized web**. We can define this as a network that **operates without a central authority**. Instead of relying on one main hub, the system distributes the work across many **independent nodes** that store and share data directly with one another. 

This aligns with **Topic C.4.8 (Decentralized and Democratic Web)** in your syllabus, which explores moving away from web monopolies toward more distributed systems.

steps.title: Transform devices into nodes
steps.content: In ConnectCo’s specific model, they solve the lack of physical infrastructure by allowing **individual devices to act as network nodes**. This means a user's phone or laptop doesn't just receive data; it helps pass data along to others. This creates a "mesh" that promotes connectivity in areas where building massive cell towers or laying fiber-optic cables isn't feasible.

highlights.text: individual devices can act as network nodes

steps.title: Address the reliability issue
steps.content: By using this decentralized approach, ConnectCo increases **reliability**. Even if the **broader internet is unavailable** (for example, a main line to the rest of the world is cut), the local nodes can still communicate with each other. This ensures that users can maintain access to local data and services regardless of the state of the central infrastructure.

steps.title: Final Review of Marks
steps.content:
To secure all 3 marks, your explanation should cover these three components:

1.  **Definition**: A web without a central authority using independent nodes. <marks>A1</marks>
2.  **Implementation**: Individual devices act as nodes to provide connectivity where infrastructure is weak. <marks>A1</marks>
3.  **Impact**: Increased reliability and local data access when the main internet is down. <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Outline one benefit and one drawback of a decentralized network model for internet services in remote areas.
parts.markscheme:
- Outline a benefit such as improved network resilience by allowing devices to connect directly, reducing reliance on traditional infrastructure. <marks>A1</marks>
- State that this allows users to access data even if one part of the network goes offline. <marks>A1</marks>
- Outline a drawback such as complicated network coordination and management, which increases the potential for data inconsistencies. <marks>A1</marks>
- Describe how decentralized models may lack sufficient security protections compared to centralized models, exposing data to risks. <marks>A1</marks>

[4 marks total]

steps.title: Analyze the question requirements
steps.content:
To answer this question using the **Reliability and Management Approach**, we need to evaluate how a decentralized network's structure affects its uptime (reliability) and the effort required to keep it running (management).

The question asks for **one benefit** and **one drawback**, worth 4 marks total. This means we need to provide a clear point and a supporting explanation for each.

highlights.text: Outline one benefit and one drawback of a decentralized network model

steps.title: Benefit: Structural Resilience
steps.content:
The primary reliability benefit of a decentralized model is the **removal of a single point of failure**. 

In a traditional centralized network, if the main hub or server fails, the entire area loses internet access. In a decentralized model, since devices connect directly to one another (peer-to-peer), the network remains operational even if several individual nodes go offline. This creates a highly resilient system suitable for remote areas with unpredictable infrastructure.

highlights.text: Outline a benefit such as improved network resilience by allowing devices to connect directly, reducing reliance on traditional infrastructure.

steps.title: Drawback: Management Overhead
steps.content:
From a management perspective, the main drawback is the **complexity of coordination and data synchronization**. 

Without a central server to dictate rules and store the "master copy" of data, it becomes very difficult to ensure all nodes are updated simultaneously. This leads to "management overhead," where significant resources are spent just trying to keep the network synchronized, increasing the risk of data inconsistencies across the different devices.

highlights.text: Outline a drawback such as complicated network coordination and management, which increases the potential for data inconsistencies.

steps.title: Final answer structure
steps.content:
To secure all 4 marks, ensure your answer is structured clearly:

*   **Benefit (2 marks):** State that decentralized networks improve resilience by removing a single point of failure, meaning the network stays active even if specific parts go offline.
*   **Drawback (2 marks):** State that management is more complex because there is no central authority, which leads to difficulties in synchronizing data and potential inconsistencies.

This topic is closely linked to **Topic 3.1: Networks** and **Option C: Web Science**, specifically regarding the evolving decentralized web.

steps.title: Analyze the decentralized model
steps.content: In a decentralized network model, especially one where individual devices act as nodes (often referred to as a mesh network), there is no single central server or authority controlling the flow of data. This relates to **Topic 3.1.1 (Types of Networks)**. We will evaluate this using the **Infrastructure and Security Approach**, focusing on physical setup costs versus the risks of distributed management.

highlights.text: decentralized model where individual devices can act as network nodes

steps.title: Outline the infrastructure benefit
steps.content:
The primary benefit in remote areas is the **reduced reliance on expensive physical infrastructure**. Traditional internet requires significant investment in fiber-optic cables or cell towers, which is often not feasible in remote regions. 

By allowing devices to connect directly to one another, the network becomes more **resilient**; if one device or path fails, the data can simply 'hop' through a different set of nodes to reach its destination. This ensures continued access to data without needing a central hub to be active at all times.

steps.title: Outline the security drawback
steps.content:
The main drawback is the **increased security vulnerability** and lack of centralized oversight. In a centralized system, a company can monitor all traffic and implement uniform security protocols (like firewalls or encryption updates) from a single point. 

In a decentralized model, every individual device is a potential point of entry for attackers. Without a central authority to manage coordination, it is much harder to ensure that every node has sufficient security protections, leaving the entire network and its data exposed to higher risks of interception or inconsistency.

steps.title: Final Review of Marks
steps.content:
To secure all 4 marks, ensure your answer covers both the 'what' (the point) and the 'why/how' (the explanation):

1.  **Benefit Point:** Reduced reliance on traditional infrastructure/improved resilience. <marks>A1</marks>
2.  **Benefit Explanation:** Users can access data even if parts of the network go offline because devices connect directly. <marks>A1</marks>
3.  **Drawback Point:** Lack of centralized oversight/management. <marks>A1</marks>
4.  **Drawback Explanation:** Lower security protections compared to central models, leading to higher data risks. <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Explain one challenge that ConnectCo may face in ensuring data consistency across decentralized nodes.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify that the lack of centralized control makes managing data consistency challenging. <marks>M1</marks>
- Explain that data updates in one node may not immediately propagate to other nodes, leading to temporary inconsistencies. <marks>A1</marks>
- Outline the requirement for complex implementation of robust real-time synchronization protocols. <marks>A1</marks>

[3 marks total]

steps.title: Identify Decentralized Challenges
steps.content: In a traditional network, a central server manages all data. However, ConnectCo's model uses individual devices as nodes. The first major challenge is that there is a **lack of centralized control**, meaning there is no single authority to dictate the "true" state of data at any given millisecond.

highlights.text: lack of centralized control

steps.title: Analyze Propagation Delay
steps.content: Using the **Propagation Delay Approach**, we look at the time it takes for an update to travel. When a user updates data on one device (Node A), that information must be sent to every other node in the global network. Because these nodes are distributed and may have unreliable connections, the **data updates in one node may not immediately propagate to other nodes**.

highlights.text: data updates in one node may not immediately propagate to other nodes

steps.title: Impact of Temporary Inconsistency
steps.content: This delay creates a window of time where different nodes hold different versions of the same data. For example, Node B might still show an old account balance while Node A shows the new one. This leads to **temporary inconsistencies** across the network, which can cause errors if two users try to modify the same data simultaneously during that lag period.

highlights.text: leading to temporary inconsistencies

steps.title: Requirement for Synchronization
steps.content: To solve this, ConnectCo cannot simply send a single message; they require a **complex implementation of robust real-time synchronization protocols** (such as gossip protocols or consensus algorithms). These protocols ensure all nodes eventually agree on the data, but managing them in an environment with "unreliable infrastructure" adds significant technical overhead.

highlights.text: complex implementation of robust real-time synchronization protocols

steps.title: Absence of Central Authority
steps.content: In a decentralized web model, the primary challenge is the **lack of a central authority** or master server. In a traditional centralized system, one server decides the 'official' state of data. Without this, there is no single 'referee' to determine which node's data is the absolute truth, making it difficult to maintain a consistent state across all participating devices.

highlights.text: lack of centralized control

steps.title: Conflict in Event Ordering
steps.content: A major technical hurdle is the **difficulty of ordering events**. Because nodes are spread out geographically and have slightly different internal clocks, if two users update the same data point simultaneously, the network cannot easily determine which update happened first. This creates a conflict where different nodes might accept different 'winners' for the same update.

steps.title: Propagation and Inconsistency
steps.content: Data updates do not travel instantly. There is a **propagation delay** as information moves from one device to another. During this lag, some nodes will hold the new data while others still hold the old data, leading to **temporary inconsistencies** (often called 'eventual consistency'). If the network infrastructure is unreliable, as mentioned in the scenario, these gaps in 'truth' can last longer.

highlights.text: data updates in one node may not immediately propagate to other nodes

steps.title: Complex Protocol Requirements
steps.content: To resolve these conflicts, ConnectCo must implement **complex synchronization protocols** (such as consensus algorithms or conflict-resolution logic). These protocols are technically difficult to design and can add significant overhead to the network, potentially slowing down the user experience in exchange for data reliability.

highlights.text: complex implementation of robust real-time synchronization protocols

steps.title: Identify decentralized control issues
steps.content: In a decentralized web model, there is no central authority or master server to manage and validate data. This lack of centralized control is a fundamental challenge because there is no single "source of truth" to coordinate which version of the data is the most recent or correct.

highlights.text: decentralized model

steps.title: Analyze node availability (Churn)
steps.content: A major challenge in this model is **node churn**, which refers to individual user devices frequently joining and leaving the network. Since ConnectCo targets areas with **unreliable infrastructure**, devices (acting as nodes) will often lose connectivity or go offline to save power.

highlights.text: individual devices can act as network nodes
unreliable infrastructure

steps.title: Explain propagation delays
steps.content: Because nodes are intermittently available, data updates cannot be broadcast to all nodes simultaneously. If an update occurs while a node is offline, that node remains outdated. This results in **temporary inconsistencies**, where different users see different versions of the same data until the node reconnects and synchronizes.

steps.title: Address synchronization requirements
steps.content: To resolve these inconsistencies, ConnectCo must implement complex **synchronization protocols** (like gossip protocols). These protocols must be robust enough to handle high latency and frequent disconnections, ensuring that data eventually propagates to every node even without a persistent connection between them.

parts.content: Describe one way open standards could support ConnectCo’s transition to a decentralized web model.
parts.markscheme:
- Describe how open standards facilitate compatibility among diverse devices, ensuring network cohesion. <marks>A1</marks>
- State that these standards allow for the use of widely accepted protocols, reducing development costs and ensuring interoperability. <marks>A1</marks>

[2 marks total]

steps.title: Define Open Standards
steps.content: To understand how open standards help ConnectCo, we first need to look at what they are. Open standards are publicly available rules and specifications that define how data is exchanged. In the context of **Topic 3: Networks**, these standards ensure that devices from different manufacturers can "speak" the same language.

steps.title: Ensure Device Compatibility
steps.content:
Since ConnectCo wants individual devices to act as network nodes, they will encounter a massive variety of hardware (different brands of smartphones, laptops, and routers). 

Open standards provide a framework for **compatibility**, ensuring that a Samsung phone can successfully pass data to an Apple laptop or a generic IoT sensor. This creates a cohesive network where any device can join and function as a node without being blocked by proprietary restrictions.

highlights.text: Describe how open standards facilitate compatibility among diverse devices, ensuring network cohesion.

steps.title: Enable Universal Interoperability
steps.content:
By using widely accepted protocols (like TCP/IP or HTTP), ConnectCo ensures **interoperability**. 

This means they don't have to spend money developing unique software for every single type of device on the planet. Instead, because these devices already follow open standards, they can interact seamlessly. This significantly reduces development costs and makes the decentralized model scalable across different regions and infrastructures.

highlights.text: State that these standards allow for the use of widely accepted protocols, reducing development costs and ensuring interoperability.

steps.title: Define Open Standards
steps.content: To understand how open standards help ConnectCo, we first need to recognize what they are. Open standards are non-proprietary, publicly available sets of rules that allow different systems to communicate. In a decentralized model, where **individual devices can act as network nodes**, having a common "language" is essential for these nodes to find and talk to one another.

highlights.text: individual devices can act as network nodes

steps.title: Ensure Interoperability
steps.content: From a development efficiency standpoint, open standards facilitate compatibility among diverse devices (like smartphones, laptops, or routers from different manufacturers). Because these devices all follow the same protocols, they can join the network and share data seamlessly. This **ensures network cohesion**, meaning ConnectCo doesn't have to spend years creating specific software for every single brand of device in the area.

highlights.text: ensure network cohesion

steps.title: Reduce R&D Costs
steps.content: Using established open standards allows ConnectCo to leverage existing infrastructure and widely accepted protocols (like $TCP/IP$ or decentralized protocols like $IPFS$). This is a major economic win because it significantly **reduces development costs**. Instead of "reinventing the wheel" and funding expensive research to create a proprietary communication method, they can use tools and libraries that are already proven and available for free.

highlights.text: reducing development costs

steps.title: Final Answer Summary
steps.content:
To get full marks on this question, you should combine the ideas of compatibility and cost-saving. 

**Final Answer:** 
Open standards allow ConnectCo to ensure compatibility between diverse devices, ensuring that the decentralized network remains cohesive. Furthermore, by using these widely accepted protocols, the company can significantly reduce its research and development costs by leveraging existing software and infrastructure.

parts.content: To what extent could a decentralized internet model impact international data accessibility?
parts.markscheme:
- State that decentralized models can bypass national censorship restrictions and firewalls. <marks>A1</marks>
- Explain that data stored across multiple locations increases availability in restricted regions. <marks>A1</marks>
- Identify that it reduces dependency on centralized service providers, promoting internet freedom. <marks>A1</marks>
- Outline that the lack of regulatory oversight raises concerns over data misuse and illegal content. <marks>A1</marks>
- State that network fragmentation makes global governance and coordination difficult. <marks>A1</marks>
- Identify that security challenges limit effectiveness for sensitive or confidential information. <marks>A1</marks>

<note>Award marks for other relevant points with clear reasoning</note>

[6 marks total]

steps.title: Analyze the impact task
steps.content: To answer "to what extent," we need a **Balanced Evaluation**. This means we'll look at how a decentralized model (where individual devices act as nodes) improves data access, while also considering the risks that could limit its effectiveness. This topic relates to **Topic C.4.8 (Decentralized and Democratic Web)**.

highlights.text: To what extent could a decentralized internet model impact international data accessibility?

steps.title: Positive: Bypassing national censorship
steps.content: In a traditional centralized model, governments can easily block access to specific servers or websites via national firewalls. In a decentralized model, because data is served by many individual nodes rather than a single central point, it becomes much harder for authorities to implement blanket censorship, significantly increasing data accessibility in restricted regions.

steps.title: Positive: Redundancy and availability
steps.content: Because data is stored across multiple locations and devices, the network has higher redundancy. If one path or node is taken offline—whether due to infrastructure failure or government intervention—the data remains accessible through other nodes. This is crucial for the "unreliable infrastructure" mentioned in the scenario.

highlights.text: unreliable infrastructure

steps.title: Positive: Reducing provider dependency
steps.content: A decentralized model promotes internet freedom by reducing the power of large, centralized Service Providers (ISPs). When users do not depend on a single gateway, they are less vulnerable to ISP-level throttling or data gatekeeping, which levels the playing field for international data access.

steps.title: Negative: Lack of regulatory oversight
steps.content: On the flip side, the absence of a central authority means there is no mechanism for content moderation. This lack of oversight can lead to the spread of illegal content, misinformation, or data misuse. Without a governing body to respond to complaints, the accessibility of "harmful" data increases alongside "helpful" data.

steps.title: Negative: Fragmentation and coordination
steps.content: Global data accessibility relies on common protocols and standards (**Topic 3.1.2**). Decentralization can lead to network fragmentation, where different groups of nodes use different protocols. This makes global governance and coordination difficult, potentially creating "islands" of data that cannot talk to each other effectively.

steps.title: Negative: Security and confidentiality
steps.content: If individual devices act as nodes, sensitive or confidential information might be processed or stored on untrusted hardware. These security challenges mean that while *public* data becomes more accessible, *secure* data accessibility might actually decrease because the risks of data breaches or unauthorized access are too high for professional or governmental use.

highlights.text: individual devices can act as network nodes

steps.title: Synthesis and final mark
steps.content:
To conclude, a decentralized model dramatically improves access by removing central points of failure and control (bypassing firewalls). However, the "cost" of this accessibility is a significant loss in security and regulatory control. 

**Marks awarded for:**
*   Bypassing censorship <marks>A1</marks>
*   Redundancy/multiple locations <marks>A1</marks>
*   Reducing dependency on providers <marks>A1</marks>
*   Lack of oversight/illegal content <marks>A1</marks>
*   Fragmentation challenges <marks>A1</marks>
*   Security/confidentiality risks <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the stakeholders
steps.content:
To evaluate the extent of the impact, we must look at how different groups are affected. This question explores the shift from a **centralized** model (where data flows through a few main points) to a **decentralized** one (where every device is a node). We will analyze the impact on:
1. **Users in restricted regions**
2. **National governments**
3. **Global service providers**

This relates to **Topic 1.2.3 (Social and Ethical Issues)** and **Topic C.4.8 (Decentralized and Democratic Web)** in your IB syllabus.

highlights.text: users in restricted regions
national governments seeking control
global service providers

steps.title: Impact: Users in restricted regions
steps.content:
For users living under strict internet controls, a decentralized model significantly increases accessibility. 

- **Bypassing Censorship:** Because there is no central server to block, these models can often bypass national firewalls and censorship. 
- **High Availability:** Since data is stored across multiple individual nodes rather than one location, it is much harder for a government to "shut down" access to specific information. 
- **Internet Freedom:** This reduces the dependency on local, state-controlled ISPs, promoting a more democratic flow of information.

highlights.text: State that decentralized models can bypass national censorship restrictions and firewalls.
Explain that data stored across multiple locations increases availability in restricted regions.

steps.title: Impact: National governments
steps.content:
From the perspective of governments, decentralization creates significant governance challenges that might actually *hinder* safe accessibility:

- **Lack of Regulatory Oversight:** Without a central authority, it is nearly impossible for governments to regulate content. This raises serious concerns regarding the spread of **illegal content** or **data misuse**.
- **Coordination Difficulties:** Global data accessibility requires some level of coordination. **Network fragmentation** (where the internet breaks into smaller, unconnected pieces) makes it difficult for nations to work together on international standards or cybercrime prevention.

highlights.text: Outline that the lack of regulatory oversight raises concerns over data misuse and illegal content.
State that network fragmentation makes global governance and coordination difficult.

steps.title: Impact: Global service providers
steps.content:
Companies like ConnectCo face a double-edged sword regarding infrastructure and security:

- **Reduced Dependency:** Decentralization removes the need for expensive, centralized data centers in every region, which can lower costs and increase the reach of their services.
- **Security Challenges:** However, because data is distributed across individual "untrusted" devices, ensuring the **confidentiality** and **integrity** of sensitive information becomes much harder. This limit in security might make the model unsuitable for banking or official government data, limiting its overall "extent" of use.

highlights.text: Identify that it reduces dependency on centralized service providers, promoting internet freedom.
Identify that security challenges limit effectiveness for sensitive or confidential information.

steps.title: Conclusion: To what extent?
steps.content:
In conclusion, a decentralized model impacts accessibility to a **great extent** by providing a technical workaround for censorship and infrastructure failure. 

However, this is tempered by the **lack of security** for sensitive data and the **legal vacuum** created by the absence of central regulation. While it empowers the individual user, it complicates the roles of the government and the provider in maintaining a safe, unified global network.

steps.title: Analyze the impact
steps.content:
To evaluate the extent to which a decentralized internet model impacts international data accessibility, we must look at how shifting from a centralized server-client model to a peer-to-peer (P2P) node-based model changes the flow of information. 

This approach will be organized into three themes: **political freedom**, **technical reliability**, and **legal/security challenges**.

steps.title: Theme: Political Freedom
steps.content:
A decentralized model significantly enhances accessibility by bypassing top-down restrictions:

*   **Bypassing Censorship:** Because there is no central gateway, it is much harder for national governments to implement firewalls or block specific IP addresses of centralized servers.
*   **Promoting Internet Freedom:** By reducing the power of centralized service providers (ISPs), the model promotes a more democratic web where information flow is determined by users rather than corporate or state entities.

highlights.text: bypass national censorship restrictions and firewalls
reduces dependency on centralized service providers

steps.title: Theme: Technical Reliability
steps.content:
The technical architecture of a decentralized system improves the physical availability of data:

*   **Distributed Storage:** In a decentralized web, data is fragmented and stored across multiple nodes (individual devices). This ensures that if one geographical region is cut off, the data remains available from other nodes globally.
*   **Resilience to Failure:** For users in areas with unreliable infrastructure (like those ConnectCo is targeting), the lack of a single point of failure means the network remains functional even if local backbone links are unstable.

highlights.text: data stored across multiple locations increases availability

steps.title: Theme: Legal and Security
steps.content:
While accessibility increases, several challenges limit the model's overall effectiveness:

*   **Lack of Governance:** Without a central authority, there is no regulatory oversight. This makes it difficult to manage illegal content (like copyright infringement or harmful data) or coordinate global standards.
*   **Security Concerns:** Because data is passed through various user-owned nodes, ensuring the confidentiality and integrity of sensitive information becomes much more complex compared to secured, professional data centers.
*   **Fragmentation:** The network can become fragmented into smaller "islands," making international coordination and data consistency difficult to maintain.

highlights.text: lack of regulatory oversight raises concerns over data misuse
network fragmentation makes global governance and coordination difficult
security challenges limit effectiveness for sensitive or confidential information

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content: In conclusion, a decentralized model impacts accessibility to a **great extent** by providing a resilient, censorship-resistant platform. However, its effectiveness is hindered by significant **governance and security risks** that make it less suitable for professional or highly sensitive data exchanges compared to the traditional web.

questions.specification:
A global media company, *Global-Media*, has launched a new high-definition video streaming service called *StreamSphere*. The platform hosts several petabytes of high-resolution media that must be delivered efficiently to millions of subscribers across different continents.

To manage this infrastructure, *Global-Media* utilizes various web technologies and delivery architectures.

parts.content:
With reference to the URL `https://dev.streamsphere.org/v2/meta-data` 

State the Top-Level Domain (TLD).
parts.markscheme: org / .org <marks>1</marks>

parts.content: With reference to the search engine discovery process, identify **three** stages involved when a search engine spider (crawler) discovers and indexes a new page on the *StreamSphere* website.
parts.markscheme:
1. The spider follows a hyperlink from an existing, already-indexed page to find the new URL; <marks>1</marks>
2. The spider downloads the page content and parses the HTML to extract text, keywords, and metadata; <marks>1</marks>
3. The parsed data is categorized and added to the search engine's database (the index) so it can be retrieved for relevant user queries; <marks>1</marks>

Note: Award up to <marks>3</marks> maximum.

parts.content:
*Global-Media* has decided to build the *StreamSphere* player using open web standards (such as HTML5 and CSS3) rather than using proprietary plugins.

Explain **two** reasons why *Global-Media* would adopt open web standards for its platform.
parts.markscheme:
**Reason 1: Cross-platform interoperability**
Open standards are maintained by organizations like the W3C and are natively supported by all major modern web browsers (Chrome, Safari, Firefox). <marks>1</marks> This ensures the streaming service is accessible on a wide variety of devices (mobile, smart TVs, PCs) without requiring users to install third-party software. <marks>1</marks> Consequently, this increases the potential subscriber base and reduces technical support issues. <marks>1</marks>

**Reason 2: Longevity and lower licensing costs**
Because open standards are not owned by a single company, they are free to use without paying royalty fees. <marks>1</marks> This prevents "vendor lock-in," where the company would be dependent on a single software provider's pricing or development roadmap. <marks>1</marks> It also ensures the content remains accessible in the long term, even if a particular software company goes out of business. <marks>1</marks>

Note: Candidates may receive marks for discussing accessibility, SEO advantages, or the lack of security vulnerabilities often associated with proprietary plugins.
